stories.title: Partnership Unveils Plans for Redevelopment of Former Brooklyn Macy’s Building
stories.excerpt: United American Land, together with partners The Jackson Group and Dreamscape Retail & Entertainment, has unveiled plans for BKX, a redevelopment of the former Macy’s building at 422 Fulton St. into one of New York City’s largest and most ambitious retail developments. Spanning approximately 440,000 square feet across five levels, BKX will represent the largest contiguous block of retail space currently available in New York City. It has been designed for flagship retail, immersive entertainment, hospitality and experiential concepts, the development team said. “When we acquired this property, we saw an opportunity to reimagine one of New York’s most iconic sites for the next generation,” said Albert Laboz, principal of United American Land. “Rather than pursuing a traditional retail redevelopment, we’re creating a destination that reflects how people want to spend time today – bringing together entertainment, dining, retail and community under one roof.” The post Partnership Unveils Plans for Redevelopment of Former Brooklyn Macy’s Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: United American Land, Jackson Group, Dreamscape Retail converting former Macy's at 422 Fulton St., Brooklyn into 440K sf retail/entertainment.
Largest contiguous retail block in NYC; flagship, immersive, experiential design; not traditional retail.
No residential component; retail-focused redevelopment does not signal multifamily demand or supply pressure.
stories.summary: A partnership led by United American Land is transforming the iconic former Macy's building at 422 Fulton Street in Brooklyn into BKX, a 440,000-square-foot retail and entertainment destination spanning five levels. The project targets flagship retail, immersive entertainment, hospitality, and experiential concepts rather than traditional retail. No timeline or unit count disclosed.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. The conversion of major retail anchors to experiential/entertainment uses in Brooklyn may indirectly support foot traffic and neighborhood vitality that benefits nearby residential, but this deal itself is non-residential.

stories.title: Kolter and Rockpoint Land $108M Loan for Palm Beach Gardens Rental
stories.excerpt: Developer Kolter Group and private equity firm Rockpoint have secured $107.8 million to build a garden-style multifamily project in Palm Beach Gardens, Fla., property records show. PNC Bank provided the construction debt for the Sutton, a five-story, 432-unit development just north of North Military Trail, west of Dixie Highway. Amenities will include a pool, a gym, coworking rooms, a resident lounge, a dog park, and a children’s playground. Earlier this week, the joint venture closed on the $30 million purchase of the 17.6-acre site that housed the Hilltop Gardens Mobile Home Park. Construction is expected to be completed in 2028. “Our selective development strategy allows us to focus on submarkets like Palm Beach Gardens that are highly supply constrained and have demand for newer vintage apartment living,” Dan Domb, a managing member and COO at Rockpoint, said in a statement. Both Rockpoint and Kolter are active players in South Florida. In June, Kolter, which focuses on residential development, nabbed a $92 million construction loan for a 386-unit, Live Local Act project in Delray Beach, where the firm is based. In February, Boston-based Rockpoint, alongside 13th Floor Investments, nabbed $100 million in debt to build a transit-oriented multifamily project in Boca Raton. The private equity firm is also a backer of Related Group’s oceanfront, 56-unit luxury condo project in Bal Harbour. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Kolter Group and Rockpoint close $30M land buy, secure $107.8M construction loan from PNC Bank for Palm Beach Gardens apartments.
The Sutton: 432-unit, five-story garden-style; 17.6 acres; opens 2028; supply-constrained submarket.
Strong capital availability and developer confidence in Palm Beach Gardens rental demand despite macro uncertainty.
stories.summary: Kolter Group and Rockpoint secured $107.8 million in construction financing from PNC Bank for The Sutton, a 432-unit garden-style multifamily project in Palm Beach Gardens, Florida. The joint venture closed the $30 million acquisition of the 17.6-acre site (formerly Hilltop Gardens Mobile Home Park) and expects completion in 2028. Amenities include pool, gym, coworking, dog park, and playground.
stories.impact: Signals strong capital availability and developer conviction in South Florida multifamily, particularly supply-constrained Palm Beach Gardens submarkets. A 432-unit delivery in 2028 will add material supply to an area characterized by limited new deliveries; absorption and rent trajectory will depend on concurrent job and population inflows.

stories.title: Feds Pay $1.5B for Two Immigration Detention Centers in California
stories.excerpt: The federal government has spent nearly $1.5 billion to acquire two California detention centers from prison operator CoreCivic, marking one of the largest real estate deals tied to the Trump administration’s expansion of immigration enforcement. The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) purchased the 1,994-bed Otay Mesa Detention Center in Greater San Diego for $739.2 million, and the 2,560-bed California City Detention Facility in Kern County north of Los Angeles for $732.6 million, CoreCivic announced. The deals closed July 2. CoreCivic anticipates it will continue to manage both detention centers under contracts with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), although those agreements can be terminated by the agency. The California City management contract runs through August 2027, while the Otay Mesa agreement extends through December 2029. The deals are a slight shift in strategy for DHS. Earlier this year, DHS paid more than $1 billion to acquire about 7.6 million square feet of warehouse space to convert into detention facilities across eight states, according to CoStar News, as opposed to buying existing detention facilities. The first industrial deals drew increased scrutiny: DHS’s Office of Inspector General launched an audit in May to evaluate if the acquisitions were a cost-effective use of taxpayer funds, and The New York Times reported last month that ICE is looking to sell some of the warehouse properties. CoreCivic also indicated there could be more sales after the deals in California. The company said it is in discussions with ICE regarding the potential sale of additional detention facilities from its national portfolio, which includes 45 correctional facilities and management contracts for nine others. But CoreCivic also said it is not guaranteed any more deals will close. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: DHS purchased Otay Mesa Detention Center (San Diego, 1,994 beds) and California City Detention Facility (Kern County, 2,560 beds) for $1.48B combined.
CoreCivic to continue management; deals reflect Trump administration immigration enforcement expansion.
Detention facility acquisitions unrelated to multifamily residential demand, supply, or development feasibility.
stories.summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security acquired two California detention centers from CoreCivic for approximately $1.48 billion: the Otay Mesa Detention Center near San Diego ($739.2M, 1,994 beds) and the California City Detention Facility in Kern County ($732.6M, 2,560 beds). CoreCivic will continue managing both under ICE contracts through 2027–2029. The purchases reflect the Trump administration's expansion of immigration enforcement capacity.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development. Federal detention facility acquisitions do not affect rental housing demand, supply pipeline, construction costs, or capital availability for residential projects.

stories.title: MMCC Arranges Financing for Northridge Offices
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation (MMCC) arranged $5.7 million in financing for a 48,954-square-foot office property located at 9221 Corbin Ave. in the Northridge neighborhood of Los Angeles. Dean Giannakopoulos, senior managing director in MMCC’s Chicago office, secured the three-year loan at 65% loan-to-value through a regional bank on behalf of a private client. Additional terms include a 6% interest rate and a 25-year amortization schedule. “This financing reflects continued lender interest in well-located Los Angeles office assets with stable tenancy and strong surrounding demand drivers,” said Giannakopoulos. “By securing competitive leverage and a fixed-rate structure through a bank, we were able to provide our client with a reliable capital solution aligned with the property’s long-term ownership strategy.” The property is located near California State University, Northridge and is occupied by a diverse mix of tenants, including a tutoring service, internet service provider, computer repair service, consultants, real estate agents and accountants. The post MMCC Arranges Financing for Northridge Offices appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap arranged $5.7M loan for 48.9K sf office building at 9221 Corbin Ave., Northridge, Los Angeles.
65% LTV, 6% rate, 25-year amortization; lender interest in well-tenanted LA office with stable demand drivers near CSU Northridge.
Office-focused; reflects selective lender appetite for stabilized office with institutional anchors, not multifamily demand signal.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation arranged $5.7 million in financing for a 48,954-square-foot office property in Northridge, Los Angeles, securing a 65% LTV loan at 6% fixed through a regional bank. The three-year, 25-year amortization deal reflects continued lender confidence in well-located LA office assets with stable tenancy near California State University, Northridge. The property houses diverse tenants including tutoring services, ISP, repair, and professional services.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily. Shows regional bank appetite for stabilized office in LA submarkets with institutional anchors, but does not signal multifamily-specific demand, supply trends, or capital constraints/availability.

stories.title: Naterra eyeing a $34M combination office and warehouse in Coppell
stories.excerpt: Naterra International, a cosmetics and beauty company, is looking to expand their operations in Coppell with a new $34 million combination headquarters and warehouse facility.  The facility is expected to span 442,010 square feet, with no additional information on how the space will be split between the office and the warehouse, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  Dallas-based Pross Design Group will be heading up the construction. The future headquarters of the company will sit at 1525 Point West Boulevard in the suburb just northwest of Dallas, and the facility should be completed around December 2027. The price of the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Naterra International (cosmetics/beauty) plans $34M combined HQ and warehouse in Coppell, Texas; 442K sf; completion December 2027.
Dallas-Fort Worth metro strengthens as beauty/personal care manufacturing hub; family-owned company expansion.
Corporate facility expansion in Coppell adds jobs and population inflow; indirect signal of white-collar demand for nearby apartments.
stories.summary: Naterra International, a family-owned cosmetics and beauty products manufacturer, is planning a $34 million combined headquarters and warehouse facility in Coppell, Texas, spanning 442,010 square feet. The facility, to be completed by December 2027, represents a consolidation and expansion of operations at a $77 per square foot cost. Naterra, which supplies Baby Magic, Tree Hut, and Splash products to Walmart, is relocating from its current Coppell headquarters.
stories.impact: Indirect positive signal for multifamily in DFW, specifically Coppell and northwest Dallas suburbs. Corporate headquarters and warehouse expansion attract management, logistics, and skilled labor; inbound talent will increase local rental demand for apartments. DFW's concentration of beauty industry HQs (Mary Kay, Sally Beauty, Cetaphil, Jack Black) suggests a broader talent-recruitment dynamic favorable to multifamily absorbers in Coppell and surrounding suburbs.

stories.title: Development Site Sale Closes Nearly Nine Years After Going Under Contract
stories.excerpt: Lee & Associates NYC has completed the $7.5-million acquisition of a 2.84-acre development site at 374 Atlantic Ave. in Oceanside, NY on behalf of Sunrise Senior Living, concluding a transaction that spanned nearly nine years. Kelly Koukou, executive managing director and principal at Lee NYC, represented Sunrise Senior Living. Marlon Matza of Strategic Realty Advisors represented the sellers, Grossman Nurseries and Breslin Development. The parties originally signed the purchase contract in 2017, expecting a typical entitlement process. Instead, the project evolved into a complex, multi-year effort involving municipal approvals, public hearings and an extended entitlement process that coincided with the COVID-19 pandemic and related legal proceedings before ultimately closing in 2026. “This transaction is a reminder that commercial real estate brokerage often requires much more than simply bringing a buyer and seller together,” said Koukou. “Over the course of nearly nine years, we worked alongside our client through a complex entitlement process, changing market conditions and unforeseen challenges, while never losing sight of the end goal. We’re proud to have helped Sunrise reach this important milestone, and we’re excited to see the project move from years of planning into development.” Sunrise Senior Living plans to develop an approximately 77,400-square-foot assisted living and memory care community on the site, with 84 residential units offering assisted living and dedicated memory care services. The post Development Site Sale Closes Nearly Nine Years After Going Under Contract appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lee & Associates NYC closes $7.5M acquisition of 2.84-acre development site at 374 Atlantic Ave., Oceanside, NY for Sunrise Senior Living.
Nine-year entitlement saga: contract signed 2017, closed 2026; COVID-19, municipal approvals, legal delays compounded permitting process.
Senior housing site; demonstrates extreme entitlement risk and capital lock-up; multifamily developers should flag municipal friction in Oceanside.
stories.summary: Lee & Associates NYC completed the $7.5 million acquisition of a 2.84-acre development site at 374 Atlantic Avenue in Oceanside, New York, on behalf of Sunrise Senior Living. The transaction, originally contracted in 2017, took nearly nine years to close due to a complex entitlement process involving municipal approvals, public hearings, COVID-19 disruptions, and legal proceedings. The property is designated for senior living redevelopment.
stories.impact: Highlights extreme entitlement risk and capital lock-up in Oceanside, NY. A nine-year path to close on a $7.5M parcel signals municipal regulatory friction that will deter and delay multifamily and senior housing development. Developers assessing Long Island submarkets should price in extended approval timelines and legal contingency costs; capital reserves and deal flexibility are critical in this jurisdiction.

stories.title: Walker Webcast: Economist Peter Linneman Says Economy is Stable
stories.excerpt: Despite persistent uncertainty surrounding inflation, gasoline prices and politics, economist Peter Linneman believes the U.S. economy remains on solid footing. During the July 8 Walker Webcast, Linneman told host Willy Walker, Chairman and CEO of Walker & Dunlop, that the economy could be performing even better if businesses and consumers had greater confidence. “Could the U.S. economy be doing better? Of course, if we didn’t have all this uncertainty,” Linneman said. The Quick Growth of AI Linneman also discussed the impact of artificial intelligence on employment, saying that he doesn’t see the technology eliminating a massive number of jobs. He argued that technological innovation has historically created new industries and occupations while eliminating others. Instead, he said that workforce changes will happen gradually, rather than generating near-term widespread job losses. But he worries that today’s AI investment boom could resemble previous periods of overinvestment, such as the housing bubble. In fact, “I think AI actually is going to slow the economy over the next couple of years because we did too much too soon,” he said, adding that he believes what’s happening in AI is that “we’re investing about four years of what we should invest in two years.” Lower Interest Rates Still Expected Interest rates remain the ongoing key variable for commercial real estate. Linneman said the Federal Reserve’s relatively restrictive policy remains appropriate, adding that inflation has largely returned to the Fed’s target when owners’ equivalent rent and volatile energy prices are excluded. He backed off his previous prediction of a 75-basis-point decrease in the federal funds rate in 2026, saying the Fed will likely cut 50 bps by the end of the year. His reasons were that temporary inflationary pressures from oil price volatility should end, which could support lower interest rates. Consumer Confidence Should Improve Consumer con
bullets: Economist Peter Linneman: U.S. economy stable but held back by uncertainty; AI investment boom may slow growth 2–3 years.
Fears AI overinvestment parallels housing bubble; workforce transitions gradual, not sudden mass job loss.
Macro uncertainty and potential AI-driven slowdown could pressure rent growth and occupancy in 2026–2028; temper near-term development starts.
stories.summary: Economist Peter Linneman, speaking on the Walker Webcast, stated that the U.S. economy remains solid but could perform better if business and consumer confidence increased. He cautioned that the current AI investment boom may slow economic growth over the next two to three years, arguing that overinvestment in AI mirrors prior boom-bust cycles like the housing bubble. He does not expect AI to cause sudden mass job losses but predicts gradual workforce transitions.
stories.impact: A potential 2–3 year slowdown driven by AI overinvestment would compress rent growth and occupancy rates across multifamily portfolios, particularly if coupled with weakening consumer confidence. Developers should stress-test underwriting for softer demand and lower NOI in 2026–2028; this argues for cautious new-start timing and a focus on supply-constrained, high-barrier-to-entry markets where fundamentals are less sensitive to macro softness.

stories.title: JLL Closes Sale of Boutique Hotel in New Hampshire Capital City
stories.excerpt: JLL’s Hotels & Hospitality Group finalized the sale of The Centennial Hotel, a 32-room Victorian-era boutique hotel located at 96 Pleasant St.t in downtown Concord, New Hampshire’s capital city. Senior managing director Alan Suzuki represented the seller, Sparta Properties, with John Linell serving as JLL’s New Hampshire broker of record for the transaction. Lord Hotels acquired the asset. The sale price was not disclosed. Built in 1898 and converted to a hotel in 1996, The Centennial Hotel sits on 1.85 acres along Pleasant Street. Between 2023 and 2024, ownership completed comprehensive capital improvements. The property serves New Hampshire’s most concentrated business and medical district. “We continue to see high-net-worth individuals and families gravitating toward boutique hotel acquisitions, particularly assets with strong stories and strategic locations like The Centennial Hotel,” said Suzuki. “This Victorian-era property offered an ideal combination of recent capital improvements, unencumbered operations and immediate upside through hands-on management and food & beverage optimization.” The post JLL Closes Sale of Boutique Hotel in New Hampshire Capital City appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL closed sale of The Centennial Hotel (32 rooms, 1.85 acres) in downtown Concord, New Hampshire to Lord Hotels; price undisclosed.
Built 1898, converted to hotel 1996, renovated 2023–2024; boutique property in downtown business/medical district.
Hotel sale; no multifamily implications. Reflects investor interest in boutique hospitality, not residential demand signals.
stories.summary: JLL's Hotels & Hospitality Group closed the sale of The Centennial Hotel, a 32-room Victorian-era boutique hotel at 96 Pleasant Street in Concord, New Hampshire's capital city, to Lord Hotels. Built in 1898 and converted to a hotel in 1996, the property sits on 1.85 acres and underwent comprehensive capital improvements between 2023 and 2024. The sale was brokered by Alan Suzuki (JLL) and John Linell, with Lord Hotels acquiring the asset; sale price was not disclosed.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. The transaction reflects high-net-worth interest in boutique hospitality assets with strong operational upside, not residential demand or supply trends.

stories.title: San Antonio closes on three key Project Marvel parcels for $30M
stories.excerpt: San Antonio formally acquired federal properties downtown that are within the boundaries of a potential sports and entertainment district dubbed Project Marvel.  Officials for the General Services Administration and the city announced Monday that San Antonio paid $30 million for the federal office building at 727 East César Chávez Boulevard and two parking lots on Indianola Street. The purchase was funded by the NBA franchise the San Antonio Spurs, city manager Erik Walsh said in a statement. The San Antonio Business Journal reported that part of Project Marvel is set aside for the Spurs, who plan on building a new […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: San Antonio closes on three federal parcels (727 E. César Chávez Blvd. federal office building, two Indianola parking lots) for $30M; funded by Spurs.
Project Marvel: $1.2–$1.5B mixed-use district; Spurs new arena, 50-acre mixed-use, 1,000-room hotel, expanded convention center.
Major downtown catalytic investment and zoning precedent; multifamily component not detailed, but large mixed-use footprint will support demand for downtown apartments.
stories.summary: San Antonio closed the acquisition of three federal properties for $30 million, funded by the NBA's San Antonio Spurs, as part of Project Marvel, a planned $1.2–$1.5 billion sports and entertainment district. The parcels include a federal office building at 727 East César Chávez Boulevard and two parking lots on Indianola Street. Project Marvel will include a new Spurs arena, a 1,000-room hotel, expansion of the Henry B. González Convention Center, and 50 acres of mixed-use development.
stories.impact: Significant positive catalyst for downtown San Antonio multifamily. A $1.2–$1.5B mixed-use district anchored by a new NBA arena will concentrate foot traffic, employment, and visitor demand, driving residential absorption in walkable downtown submarkets. Multifamily developers should prioritize pre-leasing and site control near the arena and mixed-use core; the project will reshape downtown's appeal and rents. Expect strong intra-city migration from suburbs into new downtown rental product, especially urban-focused, younger demographics.

stories.title: Arlington, Va., Hotel-to-Resi Project Sells for $35M
stories.excerpt: An extended-stay hotel in Arlington, Va., is set to be transformed back into multifamily housing. GoodHomes Communities put down $35 million to acquire the Arlington Court Suites, a 187-key hotel that the buyer plans to convert into a 180-unit apartment building. KLNB announced the deal and, together with HREC Investment Advisors, represented the seller, Arlington Hotel Holdings. The 219,000-square-foot development is at 1200 North Courthouse Road. It was built in 1963 as an apartment complex but changed to an extended-stay hotel in the 1980s. With Arlington County approving the conversion back to apartments, the sale adds to the growing number of adaptive reuse projects in the region. Similarly, the deal extends GoodHomes’ redevelopment strategy to reposition aging hospitality assets into multifamily housing. The KLNB team included Rawles Wilcox, Justin Shay and Dutch Seitz, and HREC’s team included Mark Morris and Scott Stephens. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: GoodHomes Communities acquires Arlington Court Suites (187-key hotel, 219K sf) for $35M; converting to 180-unit apartment building.
Built 1963 as apartments, converted to extended-stay hotel 1980s; Arlington County approved conversion back to residential.
Hotel-to-resi conversion adds 180 units to Arlington supply; trend of adaptive reuse expands regional multifamily inventory; strong demand signal for DC suburbs.
stories.summary: GoodHomes Communities acquired the Arlington Court Suites, a 187-key extended-stay hotel in Arlington, Virginia, for $35 million, with plans to convert it into a 180-unit apartment building. Located at 1200 North Courthouse Road and spanning 219,000 square feet, the property was originally built as an apartment complex in 1963 before converting to a hotel in the 1980s. Arlington County approved the conversion back to residential. KLNB and HREC Investment Advisors represented the seller, Arlington Hotel Holdings.
stories.impact: Signals strong multifamily fundamentals and zoning flexibility in Arlington, Virginia (DC suburbs). The conversion of an aging extended-stay hotel into 180 units of new-vintage rental apartments demonstrates developer confidence in Arlington's rental demand and investor appetite for adaptive reuse. This project will add material supply to Arlington's multifamily inventory; developers should monitor conversion activity and new deliveries to assess supply-demand balance and rent trajectory in Arlington's core and edge submarkets.

stories.title: Ideal Marketplace Renews 15K-SF Grocery Store at Chelsea’s 317 Ninth Avenue
stories.excerpt: Supermarket chain Ideal Marketplace has renewed the lease on its 14,770-square-foot store in Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood. The retail deal’s address at 317 Ninth Avenue resides between West 28th and West 29th streets, inside a United States Postal Service (USPS) property that spans the entire city block. USPS, which is actively operating at the connected 341 Ninth Avenue, owns the five-story property. Founded in 2007, Ideal Marketplace, a cooperative of independent food grocers, operates locations in New York, Florida, Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Georgia and Rhode Island, according to its website. The terms of the Chelsea location’s renewed lease are unclear, but asking rents have risen across the city’s prime retail submarkets, according to a second-quarter report by JLL. In the nearby Meatpacking District, retail asking rents averaged $329 per square foot during the period. The brokers involved in the renewal deal were also unclear. A spokesperson for Ideal Marketplace did not respond to a request for comment. The site of the store’s renewal, also known as Morgan South, is part of the city’s historic USPS distribution center, which stretches across West 29th Street to the building known as Morgan North. Morgan North was recently repositioned into a multi-use property. Although USPS leases most of its post offices, USPS is a landlord in its own right. The postal service owns 8,400 facilities nationwide, according to a 2020 report by the think tank R Street. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Ideal Marketplace renews 14,770 sf grocery store lease in Chelsea (317 Ninth Ave, between W. 28th–29th); property owned by USPS.
Lease terms undisclosed; JLL reports prime retail rents averaged $329/sf in nearby Meatpacking District.
Grocery anchors in Chelsea stabilize foot traffic and neighborhood vitality; retail lease renewals support underlying residential value, not a demand signal itself.
stories.summary: Supermarket chain Ideal Marketplace renewed its lease on a 14,770-square-foot grocery store in Manhattan's Chelsea neighborhood at 317 Ninth Avenue, located between West 28th and West 29th streets on a USPS-owned property. The lease terms were not disclosed. JLL reports that retail asking rents in the nearby Meatpacking District averaged $329 per square foot during Q2, reflecting broader rent growth across prime Manhattan retail submarkets.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Grocery anchor renewals support neighborhood stability and foot traffic, indirectly benefiting nearby residential properties, but do not signal demand for new apartments or impact supply/capital/cost dynamics in Chelsea or Manhattan.

stories.title: Chinese Camera Company Insta360 to Open Store in Times Square
stories.excerpt: Insta360, a China-based global optics and camera company, has signed its first U.S. lease for a 1,100-square-foot store at 1515 Broadway in Manhattan’s Times Square, Commercial Observer has learned. The world’s No. 1 360-degree camera brand, which is a direct competitor to the popular GoPro brand, has been used for purposes ranging from undersea exploring to documenting sustainable outer space adventures. The company expects to open its new Times Square shop in August or September, where it will feature immersive demo zones, creator-led sessions, on-site product education and marketing events. Louis Franco and Joseph Isa of Isa Realty Group represented the tenant, while Ross Berkowitz of Newmark represented the building owner, SL Green Realty. The asking rent on the two-year deal was $1,000 per square foot. Sources said the short term of the lease was at the behest of SL Green, which wants to preserve options for the property that it had hoped to convert into a casino once Paramount‘s office lease ends. SL Green has also indicated it may still continue with a hotel, entertainment and observation deck conversion plan at the property between West 44th and West 45th streets. “We approached Insta360 with the idea of opening a flagship store in Times Square because there is no better retail destination in the world to make a statement for a U.S. launch,” Franco said. “Insta360 is an innovative global brand, and 1515 Broadway gives them the visibility, energy and foot traffic that only Times Square can provide.” Insta360 has previously run successful New York pop-up events for its X series of cameras, including one at Grand Central Terminal. A spokesperson for the camera company said its new, more permanent space in Times Square will give it the opportunity to “deepen connections” made through those pop-ups and “serve as a hub for the city’s creative community.” Asian brands are focusing on Times Square due to its global tourism, video billboards and late-night shop
bullets: Insta360 (China-based 360-camera manufacturer) signs first U.S. lease: 1,100 sf at 1515 Broadway, Times Square; opening August/September.
Two-year lease at $1,000/sf; SL Green wanted short term to preserve other options (casino conversion failed).
Retail lease; immersive demo/creator events. Times Square foot-traffic benefit to nearby multifamily, but no direct supply/demand signal for apartments.
stories.summary: Insta360, a China-based optics and camera company and the world's No. 1 manufacturer of 360-degree cameras, signed its first U.S. retail lease for a 1,100-square-foot store at 1515 Broadway in Times Square. The two-year lease, negotiated at $1,000 per square foot, is expected to open in August or September. The store will feature immersive demo zones, creator-led sessions, and product education. Louis Franco and Joseph Isa (Isa Realty Group) represented the tenant; Ross Berkowitz (Newmark) represented owner SL Green Realty, which preserved lease flexibility for future conversion opportunities.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail tenant leasing in Times Square reflects retail market strength and foot traffic, which indirectly supports nearby residential absorption, but the transaction itself does not signal multifamily-specific demand, supply, or capital trends.

stories.title: Inside developer Michael Stern and his Italian investor’s breakup
stories.excerpt: A partnership between developer Michael Stern and Italian investor and entrepreneur Gianluca Vacchi is imploding, newly filed court records show. Stern, founder and CEO of JDS Development Group, teamed up with Vacchi in 2024. They formed a joint venture to invest in a $4 billion pipeline of new developments that included Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, the Dolce & Gabbana-branded 888 Brickell, the 1250 West Avenue project in Miami Beach, and a planned buyout Casablanca condo-hotel, also in Miami Beach.  Of those, only two are still owned by Stern: Mercedes-Benz Places and 888 Brickell. Both are facing separate lawsuits, including a foreclosure.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: JDS Development (Michael Stern) joint venture with Italian investor Gianluca Vacchi implodes; lawsuits over $4B pipeline, alleged Ponzi scheme.
Mercedes-Benz Places, 888 Brickell (D&G-branded) remain; 1250 West Ave, Casablanca sold/disputed; Vacchi invested $60M+, claims fraud.
Miami development pipeline stalled/disputed; capital and governance risk; severe setback for signature projects; caution on JDS portfolio absorption.
stories.summary: A partnership between JDS Development Group's Michael Stern and Italian entrepreneur Gianluca Vacchi has collapsed, triggering lawsuits alleging fraud and Ponzi-scheme conduct. The joint venture formed in 2024 to develop a $4 billion pipeline including Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, 888 Brickell (Dolce & Gabbana-branded), 1250 West Avenue (Miami Beach), and a Casablanca condo-hotel. Vacchi claims Stern misrepresented project status and diverted at least $60 million of Vacchi's investment; Stern disputes the allegations. Only Mercedes-Benz Places and 888 Brickell remain under Stern's ownership, both facing lawsuits and foreclosure risk.
stories.impact: Severe setback for Miami's luxury multifamily/condo pipeline. The dissolution of a $4B development partnership and alleged fraud will slow or stall key projects in Miami Beach and Brickell, reducing near-term supply and extending delivery timelines. Capital sources will re-evaluate JDS Development's creditworthiness and governance; joint-venture structures with high-leverage, international sponsors face increased due diligence. Multifamily investors should discount or pause acquisitions of stakes in disputed JDS projects and monitor litigation outcomes before re-entering the partnership.

stories.title: JVP’s spec office construction underway at $3B Frisco project The Mix
stories.excerpt: New York-based JVP Development broke ground on a $3 billion speculative office building in Frisco’s 112-acre The Mix development.  The building at 7725 Lebanon Road will span 110,000 square feet when completed next summer if the given timeline remains on track. The Mix itself is slated to be a decades-long project that will feature dining options, two hotels, a 9-acre park and apartment complexes.  Tim Campbell, The Mix’s head of development, told the Dallas Business Journal that JVP’s office building is a crucial step in building out the rest of the development. An adjacent Whole Foods fixture that spans 100,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: JVP Development breaks ground on spec office building (7725 Lebanon Rd, Frisco, Texas); 110K sf; $3B mixed-use The Mix project.
Completion summer 2025; adjacent 100K sf Whole Foods, 635 apartments finishing 2028, 9-acre park in progress.
Major catalyst infrastructure: spec office anchors broader mixed-use; multifamily component (635 units) will test Frisco absorption capacity and rent sustainability.
stories.summary: New York-based JVP Development broke ground on a 110,000-square-foot speculative office building at 7725 Lebanon Road within Frisco's 112-acre The Mix mixed-use development. Expected to complete in summer 2025, the building is part of a $3 billion long-term project that will also feature an adjacent 100,000-square-foot Whole Foods, dining, two hotels, a 9-acre park, and 635 apartments (completing 2028). JLL's T.D. Briggs and Blake Shipley are leading leasing efforts.
stories.impact: Positive supply and demand signal for Frisco multifamily. The Mix represents major catalytic infrastructure and mixed-use density; the concurrent delivery of 635 apartments in 2028 will test Frisco's rental absorption and pricing power. Spec office by JVP signals developer confidence in Frisco's tenant demand. Multifamily developers should monitor office pre-leasing velocity and Whole Foods opening impact on foot traffic; strong mixed-use performance and office absorption will validate 2028 apartment absorption and support rent growth. Frisco's supply-constrained submarket status may face pressure from The Mix 635-unit delivery.

stories.title: Engineering failure likely behind Pfizer project scare
stories.excerpt: Major renovations on aging buildings require delicate structural work, particularly when developers add floors, cantilever new sections or redistribute loads through an existing frame. Structural engineers say those projects demand extensive analysis to ensure existing structures can safely support new weight and altered load paths. When something is overlooked in the design, engineering or construction process, the consequences can be severe. “There’s a lot of structural analysis that has to go on. If that goes on carefully, everything should work out the way the engineers planned it,” said real estate attorney and structural engineering expert Barry LePatner. “If they fail […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pfizer HQ East 42nd St. (NYC) office-to-resi conversion: structural columns buckled, upper floors sagging on 11-story cantilevered addition.
Metro Loft and David Werner Real Estate undertaking conversion; engineering failure likely, not inherent to office-to-resi conversion type.
Structural defect during active conversion; construction cost/timeline risk; regulatory/inspection scrutiny on Manhattan conversions may tighten.
stories.summary: During renovations of the former Pfizer headquarters on East 42nd Street in Manhattan, structural columns buckled and upper floors sagged on an 11-story cantilevered addition. Metro Loft and David Werner Real Estate Investments are overseeing the office-to-residential conversion. Structural engineering experts attributed the incident to specific design, engineering, or construction process oversights rather than the conversion project type itself, noting that such projects require extensive structural analysis to redistribute loads safely.
stories.impact: Construction risk flag for Manhattan office-to-resi conversions. The structural failure and sagging floors at the Pfizer conversion will likely increase city inspector scrutiny, contingency costs, and timeline risk for similar projects. Developers underwriting Manhattan conversions should add contingency for extended structural engineering review, remedial work, and potential construction delays. Insurance and bonding costs may rise. Architectural and engineering fees should increase to account for deeper load-path analysis, especially on cantilevered or multi-story additions to aging frames.

stories.title: Uptown Theatre owner seeks $200M overhaul with bigger city subsidy
stories.excerpt: How much money is enough to renovate a classic Chicago landmark? The developers aiming to revamp the long-shuttered Uptown Theatre at 4816 North Broadway and the city of Chicago are about to find out.  Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson said that a full renovation would come with a $200 million price tag, and the majority of that funding would have to be public, according to Block Club Chicago. He went on to cite a downtown office building on LaSalle Street that received $98 million from the city in tax increment financing, while he’s been offered around half of that figure.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson seeks $200M renovation funding in Chicago; currently offered ~$50M in TIF; claims 35% TIF guidance insufficient.
2018 deal ($79M public-private) fell through when partner Farpoint left in 2021; pandemic cited as reason.
TIF cap and entitlement delays deter landmark restoration; precedent shows public subsidy constraints and risk; multifamily developers face similar TIF friction.
stories.summary: Uptown Theatre owner Jerry Mickelson is seeking a $200 million public subsidy package (majority public funding) to renovate the long-shuttered landmark at 4816 North Broadway in Chicago. The city has offered approximately $50 million in tax increment financing, falling short of Mickelson's request and the 35% TIF threshold he argues is necessary. A prior $79 million public-private partnership struck in 2018 dissolved when co-developer Farpoint Development departed in 2021, citing pandemic-related market pressures. The Chicago Department of Planning and Development notes that TIF guidance caps assistance at 30% of total project costs; recent LaSalle Street approvals averaged 35%, while Congress Theater restoration received 31% TIF assistance.
stories.impact: Underscores TIF scarcity and political constraints in Chicago's development incentive landscape. The drawn-out, stalled Uptown Theatre renovation—now seeking double the original 2018 deal subsidy—signals that landmark/complex projects face mounting public-sector friction and protracted approval timelines. Multifamily developers pursuing adaptive reuse or mixed-use projects in Chicago should expect limited TIF availability, higher gap financing costs, and extended entitlement processes. Conservative underwriting, larger equity buffers, and flexible timelines are essential in Chicago given competing municipal priorities and tight incentive pools.
